featuredSetups.label: Add as vectors
featuredSetups.description: Open a clean addition case with the head-to-tail guide visible.

setup.note: Use this to read the same complex numbers as points, vectors, and a head-to-tail sum.

featuredSetups.label: Multiply by i
featuredSetups.description: Open the multiplication case where the multiplier acts like a quarter-turn.

setup.note: Use this to see that multiplication can change direction a lot without changing size much.

paragraphs: Complex numbers become easier to trust when the algebra and geometry stay on the same plane. This bench keeps z, w, and the current result visible together so a + bi can be read as both the point (a, b) and the arrow from the origin to that point.

Addition should look like vector addition: add real parts and imaginary parts, or place one arrow head-to-tail with the other. Multiplication should look different: it changes size by multiplying magnitudes and changes direction by adding arguments, so multiplying by w scales z and rotates it at the same time.

keyIdeas: A complex number z = a + bi is one object with two views: the point (a, b) and the vector from the origin to that point.
Adding complex numbers adds their horizontal and vertical components, just like vector addition on the plane.
Complex multiplication multiplies magnitudes and adds arguments, so a multiplier near the unit circle mainly rotates while a larger or smaller magnitude also stretches or shrinks.

commonMisconception.myth: Complex multiplication is just a symbolic rule for components, so the geometric picture is optional.
correction: The algebraic rule and the geometric picture describe the same operation.
On this plane, multiplication by w changes the size of z by |w| and changes its direction by arg(w), which is why the product can be read as both a formula result and a rotated, scaled arrow.

workedExamples.title: Read the result on the plane
workedExamples.intro: Use the live plane instead of a detached worksheet. First read z and w as points or arrows, then read the result from the same geometry.

items.title: Read z + w as a head-to-tail sum
items.prompt: For the current addition view, where does z + w land, and how do the components and the head-to-tail guide show the same result?

steps.label: 1. Read z and w on the plane
2. Add the real and imaginary parts
3. Match that sum to the endpoint on the plane

applyAction.label: Show the head-to-tail sum
applyAction.observationHint: The translated copy of w ends exactly where z + w lands, so the picture and the component addition are telling the same story.

items.title: Read z · w as scale plus turn
items.prompt: In multiplication view, use w's magnitude and angle to predict where z · w should land.

steps.label: 3. Match the product to the endpoint

applyAction.label: Show multiplication as a turn
applyAction.observationHint: The product arrow should match the same scale-plus-turn story: |w| controls stretch, and arg(w) controls rotation.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a multiplication case where the product lands in the second quadrant without changing its size very much from z.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether w needs to act mostly like a rotation, mostly like a stretch, or both before you try it.
miniChallenge.answer: Choose a multiplier whose argument turns z into the second quadrant while its magnitude stays close to 1.
miniChallenge.explanation: In multiplication, the product direction comes from adding arguments and the product size comes from multiplying magnitudes.

equations.meaning: Reads one complex number as the point (a, b), or equivalently as the vector from the origin to that point.
Repackages the same point as a distance from the origin and a direction on the plane.
Explains why multiplying by w changes both the size and the direction of z.

presets.description: A clean addition case where the head-to-tail picture is easy to follow.
A unit-magnitude multiplier that rotates z by 90 degrees without changing its size.
An addition case where z and w partly cancel, so the result lands much closer to the origin.

overlays.shortDescription: Show w translated to the tip of z so addition can be read head-to-tail.
whatToNotice: The same endpoint comes from both the head-to-tail picture and from adding the real and imaginary parts.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps complex addition tied to vector addition on the plane.

overlays.shortDescription: Keep a one-unit reference circle visible.
whatToNotice: A multiplier with magnitude 1 can rotate a point strongly without changing its distance from the origin.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the size story of multiplication from the turning story.

overlays.shortDescription: Show the turn from arg(z) to arg(z · w).
whatToNotice: Multiplication changes direction by adding angles.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the argument-addition rule into a visible rotation.

noticePrompts.intro: Keep the plane, the result arrow, and the active graph visible together so the algebra stays tied to the picture.

items.text: In addition view, the result arrow lands where the head-to-tail copy of w ends, so the geometry and the component sum agree.
A multiplier near the unit circle changes direction more than size, so multiplication can look mostly like a rotation.

predictionMode.title: Predict the geometric effect
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the product should do on the plane before you switch the preset or the view.

choices.label: z should rotate about 90 degrees while keeping the same magnitude.
z should collapse to the origin.

items.explanation: The multiplier i has magnitude 1 and argument 90 degrees, so it acts like a quarter-turn without scaling the point.
items.observationHint: Turn the rotation cue on and compare the original and product arrows: the distance from the origin stays the same while the direction turns by 90 degrees.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the live plane picture, not from a detached symbol rule.

questions.prompt: What is the most accurate way to read z = a + bi on this bench?
questions.explanation: The complex plane works because the algebraic form and the geometric point describe the same object.

questions.prompt: If |w| is close to 1 but arg(w) is large, what is the best expectation for z · w?

choices.label: The product should rotate a lot while changing size only a little.
The product should always become much longer.

questions.explanation: A multiplier near the unit circle mainly rotates. A large argument means a large turn, while magnitude near 1 means little scaling.

challengeMode.title: Rotate without stretching much
challengeMode.intro: Use the plane honestly: choose w so the product turns strongly while |w| stays near 1.

items.title: Turn the product onto the positive imaginary axis
items.prompt: In multiplication view, choose w so z · w lands almost on the positive imaginary axis while the multiplier magnitude stays close to 1.
items.successMessage: You built a multiplication case where the direction changed a lot while the size changed very little. The product landed near the positive imaginary axis because w acted mainly like a rotation.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a complex plane with z, w, and the current result arrow. Sliders or dragging change the real and imaginary parts of both points, and a toggle switches between addition view and multiplication view. Optional guides can show head-to-tail addition, a one-unit reference circle, and a rotation cue for multiplication.

One graph shows how the real and imaginary parts of z + w change as the real part of w changes. A second graph shows how the real and imaginary parts of z · w change under the same sweep, so addition and multiplication can be compared as two different geometric actions on the same plane.
